title: Perturbative Analysis of Dark State Dynamics in Weakly Anharmonic Photon-Emitter Pairs
abstract: Dark states are excited quantum states that decouple from their environment in such a way that they do not emit or absorb external photons. These states are found in a variety of different open quantum systems and can be derived from the collective interactions of individual quantum emitters interacting with one another. One of the simplest model where these states exist is in a pair of dissipatively coupled harmonic oscillators described under the Bose-Hubbard model. When on-site interactions are included, these states can no longer be classified as genuine dark states since dissipation is induced in them. In this paper we study the origin of this dissipation in dark states by using weak anharmonicity as a perturbing factor. In our analysis, we find the first and second order corrections to the wavefunction and apply these corrections to the master equation in order to track the dynamics.
summary: This paper analyzes how weak anharmonicity causes dark states in coupled photon-emitters to become dissipative. By using perturbation theory, the authors show that on-site interactions create a decay path through bright states and can lead to exceptional points. The results are highly accurate for small perturbations and provide insight into the cascading decay of excitations.
main_problem: Investigating how weak anharmonicity (on-site interactions) induces dissipation in dark states that are otherwise decoherence-free in harmonic systems.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that anharmonicity breaks the symmetry of dark states, creating a decay path via coupling to bright states, and identify the emergence of exceptional points in even excitation manifolds.
method: A perturbative analysis using first and second-order corrections to the wavefunction and density matrix, validated against exact numerical evolution of the Lindblad master equation.
model_or_system: A pair of weakly anharmonic photon-emitters (such as transmons) coupled via a dissipative medium, modeled using the Bose-Hubbard Hamiltonian and a non-Hermitian effective Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Complex eigenvalues (energy and decay rates), population relaxation of the dark state, and photon readout (rate of change of total excitation number).
important_parameters: Anharmonicity (U), dissipation rate (gamma), tunneling rate (J), and excitation manifold number (N).
main_assumptions: The study assumes weak anharmonicity (U/gamma is small), negligible tunneling (J=0), and large site separation to ensure coherent interaction.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of transmons and the Bose-Hubbard model; Figure 5 compares analytical energy corrections to numerical results; Figure 6 shows population relaxation and photon readout dynamics.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the non-Hermitian Bose-Hubbard model, introduces a perturbative approach to handle anharmonicity, presents analytical results for energy and wavefunction corrections, and concludes with numerical validation and discussion of dynamics like photon bursts.
why_interesting: It provides a tractable analytical framework for understanding how nonlinearity destroys decoherence-free subspaces in open quantum systems, which is critical for designing stable quantum memories in waveguide QED.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of the considered system of a two transmons coupled to a waveguide. (a) Two transmons separated by a dis- tance of d = |x1 −x2|. At each site a microcircuit is comprised of an LC circuit and a Josephson junction which interacts to an open transmission line or waveguide. The waveguide is open ensuring photons emitted into the environment are lost from the system. (b) Analogous system described by the Bose-Hubbard model as two anharmonic oscillators coupled via dissipative coupling. Superpositions of the Fock states between the oscillators can be observed from the collective constructive and destructive interference of the transmons, leading to the creation of dark and...

FIG. 2. Eigenspectrum of the effective non-Hermitian Hamil- tonian of a pair of dissipatively coupled harmonic oscillators L = 2. Here the local basis is truncated up to maximum 6 harmonic oscillator states, translated as a maximum excita- tion of Ni = 5 per site. Two regimes can be separated above and below the red dashed line classified as the half-filling line. Below this line, the system is in a harmonic oscillator regime where the eigenspectrum is evenly separated, allowing us to classify the bright (yellow), dark (blue) and faint (green) states. Above this the eigenspectrum globes into a shape reminiscent of a corresponding qubit model.

FIG. 3. The energy spectrum of the effective non-Hermitian Hamiltonian ˆHeff for 2 anharmonic oscillators dissipatively coupled as a function of the anharmonicity U/γ ∈(0, 2.5). Panels (a)-(d) are separated in terms of their excitation manifolds from N = 2, 3, 4, 5. Starting from the harmonic oscillator spectrum (c.f. Fig. 2) an anharmonicity increases the energies coalesce with the behavior dependent on the even or odd number of excitations in the system.

FIG. 4. Decomposition of the real and imaginary portions of the energies in Eqs. (11-13) for the right eigenstates of the effective Hamiltonian for a pair of dissipatively coupled anharmonic oscillators in the N = 2 excitation manifold as anharmonicity increases. In this figure an exceptional point can be seen at the critical value of U = 2γ where the energies of the bright and dark states coalesce.

FIG. 5. Analytical perturbative correction calculated from Eq. (29) compared to the energy spectrum for the dark states of energy excitation manifolds N = 0 −→6. Analytical results (crosses) are compared to a small section of the numerical results (c.f. Fig. 3) (dots) for small perturbations where U/γ ∈(0, 0.5)
